problem_statement.Title: inherited-members option does not accept multiple class names
problem_statement.Description: The :inherited-members: option (autodoc) is treated as either a flag or a single class-name sentinel. The option parser returns a single/raw value (the string 'object' for the flag form, otherwise the raw argument) and the member-filtering logic compares class names by equality against that single value. As a result, specifying multiple class names (e.g., a comma-separated list like "list, tuple") is treated as a single string and never matches individual base class names when deciding whether members are inherited from those bases.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The option parser returns a single/raw value instead of a canonical collection of names. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, function inherited_members_option (currently returns the string 'object' when the option is present as a flag and otherwise returns the raw arg). Incorrect assumption: options.inherited_members is at most a single class-name value. Correct representation (diagnosis): the option must be normalized to a collection of class names (for example, a set of strings); the flag/no-argument sentinel that is currently the string 'object' should be represented within that same normalized type (e.g. {'object'}) so semantics and truthiness are preserved.

2) The member-filtering logic compares a class name to options.inherited_members using equality. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, class Documenter, method filter_members (inner helper is_filtered_inherited_member compares cls.__name__ == self.options.inherited_members). Incorrect assumption: options.inherited_members is a single string to compare for equality. Correct relationship (diagnosis): the filter must treat the option as a collection and use a membership test (e.g., check whether cls.__name__ is in the collection of names) when deciding whether a member originates from an ignored base class.

3) Other code sites rely on options.inherited_members being truthy/falsey without normalizing its type. Locations: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, Documenter.get_object_members and ClassDocumenter.get_object_members (they use elif self.options.inherited_members: to control whether to include inherited members). Incorrect assumption: callers treat the option only as a boolean-like flag. Correct relationship (diagnosis): normalizing the option to a non-empty collection (for the named-case) preserves truthiness, but consumers that only test truthiness implicitly depend on the option being a boolean-like value; these sites must be considered when normalizing the option so that semantics remain consistent across automodule/autoclass use.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python module with a class C that inherits from two base classes (e.g., class C(CustomBase, list): ...).

Add an autodoc directive documenting C (autoclass or automodule) and set the option :inherited-members: to a comma-separated list such as "list, tuple" (or attempt to pass multiple names through automodule).
Build the Sphinx documentation using ext.autodoc enabled.
Observe that members inherited from the listed base classes are not ignored as intended.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The :inherited-members: option accepts multiple class names (for example a comma-separated list). If any listed class is encountered in a documented class' MRO as a base, members originating from any of those listed base classes should be treated as inherited according to the option and not documented as part of the derived class. The flag/empty-argument form should remain supported (i.e., a sentinel value indicating the default behavior), and normalizing the option to a canonical collection must preserve existing truthiness semantics so other consumers that only check truthiness continue to work.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The option only behaves as a single-name sentinel or a flag. A comma-separated list (e.g. "list, tuple") is treated as one single string and never matches any class names, so members from those base classes are not ignored. Only a single class-name string or the flag form has effect.

For example, there is a class that inherits multiple base classes:
```
class MyClass(Parent1, Parent2, Parent3, ...):
    pass
```
and

```
.. autoclass:: example.MyClass
   :inherited-members: Parent2
```

How should the new `:inherited-members:` work? Do you mean that the member of Parent2 are ignored and the Parent1's and Parent3's are documented? And how about the methods of the super classes of `Parent1`?

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints: duplicated *args/**kwargs when merging description type hints
problem_statement.Description: When autodoc_typehints is set to "description" and a docstring documents varargs/kwargs using the star syntax (":param *args:" / ":param **kwargs:"), the autodoc typehint merging logic produces duplicated entries for *args/**kwargs: the docstring-supplied starred entries remain and typehint insertion adds separate non-starred entries (args/kwargs). This appears in the rendered Parameters list and in the documented __init__ signature block.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py::modify_field_list: when scanning existing docstring fields it parses the field name text with parts = re.split(' +', field_name) and uses the literal parsed token (parts[1] or the joined parts[2:]) directly as a key in the arguments dict (arguments.setdefault(parts[1], ...) / arguments.setdefault(name, ...)). Docstring varargs/kwargs are often written as '*args' or '**kwargs' in :param fields, so modify_field_list records keys '*'-prefixed (e.g. '*args', '**kwargs').

2) sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py::augment_descriptions_with_types: the same literal-parsing behavior is used to populate has_description and has_type (has_description.add(parts[1]) / has_type.add(name)). It likewise records '*'-prefixed names when docstrings use the star syntax.

These two behaviors are inconsistent with how annotation keys are recorded: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py::record_typehints stores type hints keyed by inspect.signature parameter names (annotation[param.name] = ...). inspect.signature reports parameter names without leading '*' characters (e.g. 'args', 'kwargs'). Because modify_field_list and augment_descriptions_with_types do not normalize docstring-parsed names to match annotation keys (they treat '*args' and 'args' as different keys), the merge logic believes starred parameters are undocumented and adds separate non-starred fields for the annotation keys, producing duplicated entries.
StepsToReproduce: Create a class with an __init__ that has varargs and kwargs and a docstring that documents them with starred param names, for example:
class _ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:
def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
"""Init docstring.
:param x: Some integer
:param *args: Some integer
:param **kwargs: Some integer
Configure Sphinx with autodoc and set autodoc_typehints = "description"
Build the documentation (e.g. sphinx-build) and inspect the rendered Parameters sections for the class and __init__ signature
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When autodoc_typehints = "description", documented varargs/kwargs should appear only once: the docstring-supplied starred parameter entries (e.g. '*args', '**kwargs') should be recognized as covering the corresponding annotation keys and type hints should be merged into those entries (not added as separate non-starred entries). The rendered output should show a single entry for each vararg/kwarg, including its description and typehint in the Parameters lists.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Docstring-supplied starred entries and injected non-starred entries both appear. The Parameters lists include both '*args'/'**kwargs' entries (from docstring) and separate 'args'/'kwargs' entries (added by typehint merging), producing duplicated and sometimes incomplete parameter blocks.

hints_text:
I noticed this docstring causes warnings because `*` and `**` are considered as mark-up symbols:

```
    def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
        """Init docstring.

        :param x: Some integer
        :param *args: Some integer
        :param **kwargs: Some integer
        """
```

Here are warnings:
```
/Users/tkomiya/work/tmp/doc/example.py:docstring of example.ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:6: WARNING: Inline emphasis start-string without end-string.
/Users/tkomiya/work/tmp/doc/example.py:docstring of example.ClassWithDocumentedInitAndStarArgs:7: WARNING: Inline strong start-string without end-string.
```

It will work fine if we escape `*` character like the following. But it's not officially recommended way, I believe.

```
    def __init__(self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) -> None:
        """Init docstring.

        :param x: Some integer
        :param \*args: Some integer
        :param \*\*kwargs: Some integer
        """
```

I'm not sure this feature is really needed?

problem_statement.Title: rst_prolog causes first-line roles (e.g. :mod:`...`) to be treated as docinfo and break top-level headings
problem_statement.Description: When rst_prolog is set, files whose first heading line is a role or domain markup (for example ':mod:`mypackage`' followed by a section underline) do not render the heading correctly and the heading is omitted from the toctree. The preprocessing that inserts rst_prolog misidentifies role/domain lines as docinfo fields and inserts the prolog after them, which breaks docutils' title detection.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Overly-permissive docinfo detection in sphinx/util/rst.py: the module-level regex docinfo_re is defined as re.compile(':\w+:.*?') (sphinx/util/rst.py). That encodes the assumption that any line beginning with ':word:' is docinfo. This assumption is incorrect because inline roles/domain markup (e.g. ':mod:`name`') also begins ':mod:' but is not a docinfo field. 2) prepend_prolog(content, prolog) in sphinx/util/rst.py uses docinfo_re.match(line) to count leading docinfo lines and then inserts rst_prolog after those lines (and inserts generated blank lines). When a role line like ':mod:`mypkg`' is misclassified as docinfo, the prolog is inserted after it and a blank line is added; this breaks adjacency of heading text and underline and prevents docutils from recognizing the top-level section title. 3) Docinfo field list entries in reST are of the form ':fieldname: value' (i.e. there is whitespace after the second colon). Therefore docinfo detection should require whitespace after the second colon rather than matching any ':word:...'. The corrected detection can be represented by the regex anchored to start-of-line and requiring whitespace, for example: docinfo_re = re.compile(r'^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\s').
StepsToReproduce: sphinx-quickstart --no-sep --project mypackage --author me -v 0.1.0 --release 0.1.0 --language en docs

The rst_prolog should be inserted in a way that does not prevent docutils from recognizing the document's title.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When rst_prolog is set and the document's first heading line is a role (':mod:`...`'), the heading is not recognized as a top-level section: it does not render correctly and does not appear in the toctree. Investigation shows that prepend_prolog treats the role line as a docinfo field (because docinfo_re matches ':mod:`...`'), inserts rst_prolog after that line and inserts blank lines, and that breaks title detection.

problem_statement.Title: autosummary: module template 'members' contains imported members despite autosummary_imported_members=False
problem_statement.Description: When autosummary generates module templates, the template context variable 'members' contains imported members (for example 'os') even if the configuration autosummary_imported_members is set to False. This causes generated stubs/templates that iterate 'members' to show imported modules and other imported objects that should be hidden when imported members are disabled.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) In generate_autosummary_content() (sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py) the template context key 'members' is assigned the raw directory listing of the object without consulting the imported_members flag. Concretely, in the module branch the code does: ns['members'] = dir(obj) (generate.py, line where module branch builds ns). This unconditional assignment means templates will see all names in dir(module) including imported modules/objects, regardless of the autosummary_imported_members config.

2) The same unconditional-members pattern also appears in the class branch of generate_autosummary_content(): ns['members'] = dir(obj) (generate.py, class branch). In the class branch the subsequent get_members(...) calls are invoked without passing the imported flag (they rely on the default imported=True), so class member lists are likewise not consistently filtered by the imported-members concept. Both branches therefore exhibit the same root-cause pattern: providing templates an unfiltered dir() listing while other parts of the code use imported-members filtering.

3) The function get_members(...) in the same file implements a filtering heuristic that does honor the imported flag (it uses: if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__: to decide whether to include each typed member). That helper is used to populate typed lists (functions, classes, exceptions) in the module branch and is passed imported=imported_members there, but the general 'members' variable exposed to templates is not derived via get_members and so does not benefit from that filtering. This inconsistency between get_members (which honors imported_members) and the unconditional ns['members'] assignment is the direct cause of the reported behavior.

Files/locations implicated (equal weight):
- sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py: generate_autosummary_content() — the assignment ns['members'] = dir(obj) in the module branch (and the identical assignment in the class branch) leaves the template variable 'members' unfiltered and therefore ignores the imported_members flag passed into the function.
- sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py: process_generate_options() reads app.config.autosummary_imported_members and passes it into generate_autosummary_docs (so the config is propagated to generate_autosummary_content); this confirms the flag is present but the generate_autosummary_content implementation does not consistently apply it to ns['members'].
StepsToReproduce: Create a module example.py with: import os
Create a module template file that iterates the 'members' template variable (for example the default autosummary module.rst template that does a for loop over members).
Set conf.py with autosummary_generate = True and autosummary_imported_members = False
Run Sphinx autosummary generation (e.g. build docs or run the autosummary_generate step).
Open generated/example.rst and observe that 'os' appears in the members list even though autosummary_imported_members is False.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When autosummary_imported_members is False, the 'members' template variable for module templates should not include imported members (for example 'os' imported into the module). Module metadata (dunder names such as __file__, __name__, __doc__, etc.) should remain available in the template context.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Generated module templates expose the full dir(module) in the template variable 'members' (including imported modules and imported objects) even when autosummary_imported_members is False; e.g. 'os' appears in generated/example.rst.

problem_statement.Title: Napoleon emits visible backslash for trailing underscore in .. attribute:: (hello_ -> hello\\_ in HTML)
problem_statement.Description: When Napoleon renders an attribute whose name ends with an underscore (for example hello_), the generated HTML displays a visible backslash (hello\\_) because the docstring parser inserts a backslash-escaped underscore (r'\\_') into the directive argument text (".. attribute:: hello\\_"). That escaped sequence then appears literally in the rendered HTML.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs (sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py) unconditionally escapes a trailing underscore by replacing name.endswith('_') with name[:-1] + r'\_'. This produces escaped names like "hello\\_" at RST-generation time (see docstring.py lines where the function does: if name.endswith('_'): name = name[:-1] + r'\_').

2) GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section (sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py) inserts the escaped name verbatim into an attribute directive header: lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name). Because the escaped text r'\\_' is included directly in the directive argument, the backslash becomes visible in the final HTML render (the directive header context does not remove that backslash escape in the way inline fields do).

3) GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section (sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py) similarly inserts the escaped name verbatim into a method directive header: lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name). This reproduces the visible-backslash problem for method headers when method names end with an underscore.

4) NumpyDocstring (sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py) uses the same escaping and the same directive-emission code paths (it calls _escape_args_and_kwargs and inherits _parse_attributes_section/_parse_methods_section), so it is affected by the same root cause.

5) The incorrect assumption encoded by the code is: "the escaping produced by _escape_args_and_kwargs (r'\\_') is appropriate in all output contexts (inline field roles and directive headers)." That assumption is false because directive header arguments (e.g., the string placed after ".. attribute::" or ".. method::") end up showing the backslash literally in the final HTML when the escaped name is used verbatim. The exact wrong hardcoded value involved is r'\_'.
StepsToReproduce:
Create a package with empty __init__.py and a.py containing:

class A:
    """
    Attributes
    ----------
    hello_: int
        hi
    """
    pass
Run sphinx-quickstart, add 'sphinx.ext.autodoc' and 'sphinx.ext.napoleon' to extensions in conf.py.
Add ".. autoclass:: a.A" to index.rst.
From project root run: PYTHONPATH=. make clean html
Open _build/html/index.html and observe the attribute name shown as "hello\\_" (backslash visible) instead of "hello_".
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated HTML should display the attribute name with a trailing underscore as hello_ (no visible backslash). The RST directive header produced for an attribute or method should include the raw name (no backslash-escaped trailing underscore) so that Sphinx renders the trailing underscore normally in the final HTML.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated HTML shows a visible backslash before the trailing underscore (hello\\_), because Napoleon emitted an escaped underscore (\\_) into the directive argument and that backslash appears literally in the rendered HTML.

problem_statement.Title: Duplicate "Symbols" sections in generated HTML index for mixed ASCII/Unicode symbol-leading entries
problem_statement.Description: When index entries begin with different symbol characters (some ASCII like '@', others non-ASCII like '£' or '←'), the generated HTML index shows two separate sections both titled "Symbols". Both sections use the same HTML anchor, so the top-of-page index contains two "Symbols" links that both jump to the first section.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Wrong sentinel used for sorting alphabetic keys in index creation (sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py :: IndexEntries.create_index). The code prefixes alphabetic/underscore keys with chr(127): lckey = chr(127) + lckey (and, for subentries, key = chr(127) + key). That encodes the assumption that codepoint 127 sorts after all symbol characters. Many Unicode symbol characters (for example '£' U+00A3 and '←' U+2190) have codepoints greater than 127, so some symbol-leading entries end up sorted after letters while ASCII symbols (e.g. '@' U+0040) sort before letters. This causes symbol entries to be split into multiple non-contiguous blocks.

2) Grouping by consecutive keys (sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py :: IndexEntries.create_index). After sorting, create_index uses itertools.groupby(newlist, keyfunc3) where keyfunc3 maps any entry whose first character is not alphabetic and not '_' to the localized label _('Symbols'). Because the sort can produce two separate symbol blocks (one before letters and one after), groupby yields two separate groups that both map to the same displayed label 'Symbols'.

3) The HTML builder and templates use the create_index keys directly as section labels/anchors and (when splitting the index) per-key page names (sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py :: StandaloneHTMLBuilder.write_genindex; sphinx/themes/basic/genindex.html and genindex-single.html). The duplicated label values produced by create_index are rendered as identical id attributes (e.g. <h2 id="{{ key }}">) and as identical link targets in the jumpbox/sidebar (e.g. <a href="#{{ key }}"> and pathto('genindex-' + key)). As a result, the top-of-page index shows "Symbols" twice and both links resolve to the same anchor (the first occurrence), and split-index page filenames/handlers would be generated twice using the same key.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Sphinx project with an index (genindex) enabled.
Add index entries whose first character is '@' (ASCII symbol) and others whose first character is a non-ASCII symbol such as '£' or '←'. For example, entries '@foo', 'apple', '£pound', '←left'.
Build the HTML output (sphinx-build -b html . _build/html).
Open the generated index page (genindex.html) and inspect the sections and the top-of-page index links.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: All entries whose displayed index key begins with a symbol character appear under a single "Symbols" section (one heading). The top-of-page index contains a single "Symbols" link that points to that single section. If per-key pages are generated, only one per-key page for the 'Symbols' key is produced.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: There are two separate sections both titled "Symbols": the first contains entries for some symbols (e.g. '@' entries) before the regular letter sections, the second contains other symbols (e.g. '£', '←') after the letters. Both sections use the same HTML anchor, so the top index shows two "Symbols" links that both jump to the first section.

problem_statement.Title: man builder places generated pages in a flat man/ directory instead of section subdirectories (MANPATH-incompatible)
problem_statement.Description: When building man pages Sphinx writes all generated files into a single directory <build-dir>/man (flat layout). Unix man expects section subdirectories (man1, man3, ...) under a MANPATH entry. Because Sphinx does not create/use per-section directories, MANPATH-based lookups do not find the generated pages.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/builders/manpage.py::ManualPageBuilder.write constructs the output filename as targetname = '%s.%s' % (name, section) and then passes destination_path=path.join(self.outdir, targetname) to docutils.io.FileOutput (see sphinx/builders/manpage.py:83–87). This encodes the incorrect assumption that man pages belong directly in the builder outdir (flat layout) rather than in per-section subdirectories.

2) sphinx/builders/manpage.py::ManualPageBuilder.write does not create per-section directories. The write method sets docsettings.section and then writes the generated output to a file path under self.outdir, but contains no call to ensuredir or other directory-creation helper; therefore the builder never creates man1/man3/... subdirectories (the code that decides on filesystem placement is in this single write method).

3) Several tests encode and depend on the flat-directory expectation. For example, tests/test_build_manpage.py asserts files at (app.outdir / 'sphinxtests.1') and (app.outdir / 'python.1') (tests/test_build_manpage.py:19–23, 34–36), and tests/test_smartquotes.py reads (app.outdir / 'python.1') in multiple tests (tests/test_smartquotes.py:32–37, 86–92). These tests therefore encode the current flat layout and will need to expect section subdirectories if the on-disk layout is changed.

4) sphinx/writers/manpage.py::ManualPageTranslator correctly uses the configured manual_section value (self._docinfo['manual_section'] set from settings.section and inserted into the .TH header; see sphinx/writers/manpage.py:100–106 and 123–128). The translator does not contain filesystem-layout code and is not the origin of the flat-layout behavior; it only controls the generated manpage content header.
StepsToReproduce: Create or use a Sphinx project with conf.py containing a man_pages entry (for example: man_pages = [('sphinxtests', 'sphinxtests', 'Sphinx tests', ['Author'], 1)])
Run: sphinx-build -b man <sourcedir> <builddir>
Inspect the build output (for example ls <builddir>/man). Observe files like 'sphinxtests.1' and 'python.1' directly under <builddir>/man (no man1/ subdirectory).
Optionally, set MANPATH to include <builddir> and run man <name> — the man binary will not find the page because it looks for section subdirectories such as man1/.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated man pages are placed into per-section subdirectories under the builder outdir (for example <build-dir>/man/man1/<name>.1, <build-dir>/man/man3/<name>.3, ...). This layout is compatible with MANPATH so the man program can find pages when pointed to the build directory.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: All generated man pages are written directly into <build-dir>/man as <name>.<section> (flat layout). No man1, man3, etc. subdirectories are created, so MANPATH-based man lookups fail to discover the pages.

hints_text:
I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory. The build directory is not an appropriate directory to manage man pages. So no section directory is needed, AFAIK. I don't know why do you want to set `MANPATH` to the output directory. To check the output, you can give the path to the man file for man command like `man _build/man/sphinx-build.1`. Please let me know your purpose in detail.
From a [separate github thread](https://github.com/flux-framework/flux-core/pull/3033#issuecomment-662515605) that describes the specific use case in some more detail:
> When run in a builddir, `src/cmd/flux` sets `MANPATH` such that `man flux` will display the current builddir version of `flux.1`. This is done so that documentation matches the version of Flux being run.

Essentially, we are trying to make running in-tree look as similar to running an installed version as possible.

---

> I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory.

On `make install`, we do have the automake setup to copy the manpages to `$prefix/man/man1`, `$prefix/man/man3`, etc.  This did require some extra work though, since each source file and its destination has to be explicitly enumerated in the automake file.  If the man pages were built into their respective sections, a recursive copy would work too.  Not a huge deal, but just another factor I wanted to bring up.
Understandable. +1 to change the structure of output directory. As commented, it causes a breaking change for users. So I propose you to add a configuration `man_make_section_directory = (True | False)` for migration. During 3.x, it defaults to False, and it will default to True on 4.0 release. What do you think?

>I’m happy to take a crack at implementing a fix, though this change in behavior may break some people who expect everything to appear in a man/ directory.

It would be very nice if you send us a PR :-)

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints ignored for overloaded callables
problem_statement.Description: When documenting callables that use typing.overload, the autodoc_typehints configuration option is not applied to the overload signatures. As a result, type annotations appear in rendered overload signatures even when autodoc_typehints is set to hide them.
problem_statement.RootCause:
All of the overloaded-signature rendering paths in sphinx.ext.autodoc construct output by calling sphinx.util.inspect.stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) but do not ensure that kwargs reflect the autodoc_typehints configuration. Concretely:

1) FunctionDocumenter.format_signature (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py) — when a function has overloads the code iterates analyzer.overloads and for each overload calls stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) (call site: stringify_signature at line 1269). The overloaded branch does not set kwargs.setdefault('show_annotation', False) when self.env.config.autodoc_typehints is 'none' or 'description', so stringify_signature's default (show_annotation=True) causes annotations to be shown.

2) MethodDocumenter.format_signature (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py) — for overloaded methods the code iterates analyzer.overloads and calls stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) (call site: stringify_signature at line 1917). The overloaded branch similarly lacks application of the autodoc_typehints-based default for show_annotation.

3) ClassDocumenter.format_signature (sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py) — for overloaded constructors/methods the code iterates signatures returned by get_overloaded_signatures() and calls stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) (call site: stringify_signature at line 1488). This branch also does not apply autodoc_typehints defaults before rendering.

Additional supporting facts:
- The non-overloaded signature formatting paths (format_args) in FunctionDocumenter, MethodDocumenter and ClassDocumenter do apply the autodoc_typehints-based default (they call kwargs.setdefault('show_annotation', False) when autodoc_typehints is 'none' or 'description'; see FunctionDocumenter.format_args line 1211, MethodDocumenter.format_args line 1831, ClassDocumenter.format_args line 1452). This indicates the configuration is respected for regular signatures but not for overload-rendering branches.
- stringify_signature is defined in sphinx/util/inspect.py with show_annotation default True (def stringify_signature(..., show_annotation: bool = True, ...), line 549). Because the overloaded branches do not set show_annotation, annotations are printed by default.
- Overload signatures originate from the parser and ModuleAnalyzer (parser.py stores AST-derived inspect.Signature objects in parser.overloads; ModuleAnalyzer.parse assigns parser.overloads to analyzer.overloads). The representation of those signatures is correct; the observed issue is in how they are rendered (the rendering depends on the show_annotation flag passed to stringify_signature).
StepsToReproduce: In conf.py set: autodoc_typehints = 'none'
Create a module (example.py) with overloaded callables, for example using typing.overload for multiple signatures and a single implementation:
from typing import overload
@overload
def foo(x: int) -> int: ...
@overload
def foo(x: float) -> float: ...
def foo(x): return x
In an rst file include the module with automodule and :members:, then build the Sphinx docs (e.g. make html)
Inspect the generated documentation for foo: overload signatures show type annotations despite autodoc_typehints='none'
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Rendered documentation should obey autodoc_typehints for overloaded callables: when autodoc_typehints is 'none' annotations must not appear in overload signatures; when 'description' annotations must not appear in overload signatures but type information should be available in the generated description; when 'signature' annotations must appear in signatures.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Overload signatures include type annotations regardless of the autodoc_typehints setting (for example, annotations remain visible when autodoc_typehints='none' or 'description').

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_type_aliases not applied to module-level variables and class attributes
problem_statement.Description: The autodoc_type_aliases mapping from conf.py is not applied when autodoc documents module-level variables and class attributes. Forward-referenced or aliased annotation names (for example, 'String') remain unresolved or are printed as the raw annotation string for variables/attributes.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: DataDocumenter.add_directive_header — the code obtains evaluated annotations with get_type_hints(self.parent) (no autodoc_type_aliases argument) and then emits the :type: line from the result. This call omits the user-configured alias mapping, so forward refs/aliases provided via autodoc_type_aliases are ignored. (See get_type_hints(self.parent) at sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py lines ~1704–1706.)

2) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: AttributeDocumenter.add_directive_header — identical problem to (1): it calls get_type_hints(self.parent) without passing the autodoc_type_aliases mapping. This omission causes class attribute annotations to be evaluated without the user-configured alias mapping. (See get_type_hints(self.parent) at sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py lines ~2095–2097.)

3) Fallback behavior in both add_directive_header implementations: when evaluation raises NameError the code falls back to self.parent.__annotations__ (raw annotation objects/strings) or to analyzer.annotations (string forms captured from source). Those fallback annotation values are not evaluated with autodoc_type_aliases and therefore do not resolve alias mappings; this fallback path preserves the observable symptom when evaluation was not performed with the alias mapping. (See the except NameError handling and subsequent use of analyzer.annotations in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py.)

4) Inconsistency with other documenters: signature and parameter handling for functions/methods/classes uses Sphinx's inspect helpers and explicitly passes type_aliases=self.config.autodoc_type_aliases into signature/evaluate_signature calls (for example inspect.signature(..., type_aliases=self.config.autodoc_type_aliases) and typing.get_type_hints(subject, None, type_aliases) in sphinx/util/inspect.py). That is why function/method signatures respect autodoc_type_aliases while module-level data and class attributes (handled by DataDocumenter and AttributeDocumenter) do not.

5) Additional related location sharing the same omission pattern: sphinx/util/inspect.py: Signature.__init__ currently sets self.annotations = typing.get_type_hints(subject) without supplying a type_aliases mapping. This is an additional place in Sphinx code where evaluated annotations would ignore autodoc_type_aliases (see typing.get_type_hints(subject) at sphinx/util/inspect.py lines ~720–723).
StepsToReproduce:
Create example.py with:
from __future__ import annotations

#: blah blah blah
var: String

class MyString:
    "mystring"

    #: blah blah blah
    var: String

Create index.rst with:
.. automodule:: example
   :members:
   :undoc-members:
Run Sphinx to build the documentation (e.g. sphinx-build -b html <srcdir> <outdir>) and inspect the generated documentation for example.var and example.MyString.var
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When autodoc documents module-level variables and class attributes, the displayed type information should reflect the autodoc_type_aliases mapping from conf.py (forward-referenced names like 'String' should be resolved according to the mapping and the emitted :type: should show the resolved/aliased type).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For module-level variables and class attributes autodoc does not apply autodoc_type_aliases: annotations remain as the original quoted name or as the raw source annotation string (for example, 'String' is shown rather than resolving to example.MyString).

problem_statement.Title: Sphinx 3.3: "no number is assigned for table" warnings caused by mismatch in toc_fignumbers shape (singlehtml)
problem_statement.Description: After upgrading to Sphinx 3.3, builds that resolve :numref: (single-file HTML builds that use SingleFileHTMLBuilder and any build that replaces env.toc_fignumbers with an assembled single-doc mapping) may emit warnings like "WARNING: no number is assigned for table: <label>". The warnings occur at numref resolution time because the runtime mapping structure used to look up figure/table/section numbers (env.toc_fignumbers / env.toc_secnumbers) has a different shape when assembled for single-file output than the shape that the std and math domains expect.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Single-file builder shape change (producer): SingleFileHTMLBuilder.assemble_toc_fignumbers and assemble_toc_secnumbers create a single top-level mapping keyed by the master document with inner alias keys constructed as the format "%s/%s" % (docname, figtype_or_id). Concretely, the single-file shape is { master_doc: { "<docname>/<figtype_or_id>": { "<id>": fignum_tuple, ... }, ... } } (see sphinx/builders/singlehtml.py where alias = "%s/%s" and the method returns {self.config.master_doc: new_fignumbers}).

2) Consumer expectation mismatch (consumer): StandardDomain.get_fignumber assumes the per-document nested mapping env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][figure_id] (and for sections env.toc_secnumbers[docname][anchorname]). It directly indexes env.toc_fignumbers and env.toc_secnumbers by the original docname and raises ValueError when those per-doc keys are missing (see sphinx/domains/std.py: get_fignumber uses env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][figure_id] and raises ValueError on KeyError/IndexError).

3) Math domain also assumes per-doc shape: MathDomain.resolve_xref directly indexes env.toc_fignumbers[docname]['displaymath'] to assemble an equation number, so it similarly expects the per-doc mapping (see sphinx/domains/math.py). 

4) Exact failure -> visible warning: _resolve_numref_xref calls get_fignumber in a try/except; when get_fignumber raises ValueError (because the docname key is absent in env.toc_fignumbers/toc_secnumbers), the exception is caught and the message WARNING: no number is assigned for %s: %s (figtype, labelid) is logged — this is the user-visible warning (see sphinx/domains/std.py where the ValueError is caught and logger.warning(...) is emitted).
StepsToReproduce: Use Sphinx 3.3.
Create a multi-document project with a labeled table (or figure) and a :numref:`label` reference pointing to it.

Build the docs with the single-file HTML builder (sphinx-build -b singlehtml <src> <out>), which causes SingleFileHTMLBuilder to assemble env.toc_fignumbers/toc_secnumbers into a master_doc -> alias_map shape.
Observe the build output for warnings like: WARNING: no number is assigned for table: <labelid> and note that the :numref: output is not rendered with the expected number.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: References created with :numref: to tables/figures/sections resolve to the correct numeric labels (e.g. "Table 1.2") in single-file builds (singlehtml) and in other builds, without producing "no number is assigned for ..." warnings.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During reference resolution the domain code logs warnings: "WARNING: no number is assigned for table: <labelid>" and the :numref: references appear without their correct numeric labels (they are unresolved or empty). This happens when env.toc_fignumbers/toc_secnumbers have been replaced by the single-master alias mapping (master_doc -> { "<docname>/<figtype_or_id>": {id: number}}) while the consumer code still expects per-doc mappings.

hints_text: I digged into this a little bit more and it seems like the `id` of the table isn't properly making it into `env.toc_fignumbers`. If I set `:name: mylabel`, regardless the I see something like this in `env.toc_fignumbers` ``` 'pagename': {'table': {'id3': (1,)}, ``` So it seems like `id3` is being used for the table id instead of `mylabel` @choldgraf I suspect it's related to this: https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/commit/66dda1fc50249e9da62e79380251d8795b8e36df. Oooohhh good find! 👏👏👏 Confirmed that this was the issue - we had been referencing Tables that didn't have a title with `numref`, and this bugfix (I guess it was a bugfix?) caused us to start raising errors. Perhaps this restriction about tables needing a title could be documented more clearly? The `numfig` option has been described as follows. >If true, figures, tables and code-blocks are automatically numbered if they have a caption. https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/configuration.html#confval-numfig It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. > It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? I could understand such restrictions for Jupyter Book but it doesn't make a lot of sense for Sphinx IMO. I think Sphinx should allow users to have enumerable nodes with no caption. What do you think @choldgraf? >This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? Yes. Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. I don't know how many people want to assign numbers to non-captioned items. But this is the first feature request, AFAIK. I think my take is that I don't think it is super useful to be able to have numbered references for things that don't have titles/captions. However, it also didn't feel like it *shouldn't* be possible, and so I assumed that it was possible (and thus ran into what I thought was a bug). I think it would be more helpful to surface a more informative warning like "You attempted to add a numbered reference to a Table without a title, add a title for this to work." (or, surface this gotcha in the documentation more obviously like with a `warning` or `note` directive?) @tk0miya @choldgraf both make good points for restricting `figure` and `table` directives with no caption. My issue is that this is done at the enumerable node which implies that **all** enumerable nodes with no title/caption are skipped - not just `figure` and `table`. > Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. Just to clarify, `numfig` feature has - prior to v3.3.0 - supported uncaptioned tables but it did not display the caption. The user was able to reference the table using `numref` role (see example below). In the event that the user tried to reference the caption (aka `name` placeholder), Sphinx threw a warning indicating that there was no caption. This solution seemed sensible to me because it allowed other extensions to utilize enumerable nodes regardless of caption/no caption restriction. My main motivation for wanting to revert back or restrict the bugfix to tables and figures is because both the extensions I've worked on depend on the utilization of enumerable nodes regardless of captions/no captions. I think it wouldn't be too difficult to add the information to `env.toc_fignumbers` but I wanted to make a case before I addressed this in [sphinx-proof](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-proof) and [sphinx-exercise](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-exercise). **Example** Sphinx Version - v3.2.1 ````md ```{list-table} :header-rows: 1 :name: table1 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table1`. ```{list-table} Caption here :header-rows: 1 :name: table2 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table2`. ```` So it sounds like the `tl;dr` from @najuzilu is that in other extensions, she is *using* the fact that you can reference non-captioned elements with a number, and that Sphinx now removing this ability is breaking those extensions. Is that right? That's correct @choldgraf This is a screenshot of the PDF that is generated from @najuzilu 's example with v3.2.1. As you see, it does not work correctly in LaTeX output. I'd not like to support assigning numbers to no captioned items until fixed this (if somebody needs it).

problem_statement.Title: Sphinx 3.2 rejects option:: tokens with nonstandard characters (e.g. "[enable=]PATTERN")
problem_statement.Description: The std domain's option:: directive parsing treats certain option tokens as malformed and emits a "Malformed option description" warning. An example token used in QEMU docs, "[enable=]PATTERN", is rejected by Sphinx 3.2 even though earlier Sphinx versions accepted it. The warning is produced while parsing option directive signatures and causes builds to fail where warnings are treated as errors.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The file-level regular expression option_desc_re in sphinx/domains/std.py encodes an overly-restrictive assumption about valid option tokens: option_desc_re = re.compile(r'((?:/|--|-|\+)?[^\s=[]+)(=?\s*.*)') forbids '[' and '=' from being part of the captured option name (the negated character class excludes '[' and '='). (Location: sphinx/domains/std.py, option_desc_re.)

2) Cmdoption.handle_signature treats a regex non-match as a malformed option and emits a "Malformed option description" warning and skips that token. That behavior encodes the assumption that any token not matching the RE is invalid and therefore rejects previously-accepted nonstandard option syntaxes (for example "[enable=]PATTERN"). (Location: sphinx/domains/std.py, class Cmdoption, method handle_signature.)

3) The warning message logged in the same code documents a very limited set of accepted token formats ("should look like \"opt\", \"-opt args\" ..."), reinforcing the restrictive assumption. Because non-matching tokens are skipped, such tokens are not recorded in the std domain index and cannot be referenced. (Location: sphinx/domains/std.py, logger.warning call in Cmdoption.handle_signature.)
StepsToReproduce: Create an rst file that documents an option using a token such as '[enable=]PATTERN', for example:
.. option:: [enable=]PATTERN
Build the docs with Sphinx 3.2 (or newer) and warnings treated as errors (or inspect warnings).
Observe the warning: "Malformed option description '[enable=]PATTERN', should look like ..." and that the token is skipped (it will not be recorded in the std domain index).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Sphinx should accept option:: directive tokens such as '[enable=]PATTERN' (and other nonstandard option tokens that are arbitrary non-whitespace strings) without emitting a "Malformed option description" warning; the option should be recorded in the std domain index so it can be referenced.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Sphinx 3.2 emits a "Malformed option description '[enable=]PATTERN'" warning and skips that token; documentation that previously built now fails when warnings are treated as errors and the option is not present in the std domain index.

hints_text:
I disagree with 

> Sphinx ideally shouldn't change in ways that break the building of documentation that worked in older versions, because this makes it unworkably difficult to have documentation that builds with whatever the Linux distro's sphinx-build is.

The idea that things shouldn't change to avoid breaking is incredibly toxic developer culture. This is what pinned versions are for, additionally, you can have your project specify a minimum and maximum sphinx as a requirement.
I agree that there's some philosophical differences at play here. Our project wants to be able to build on a fairly wide range of supported and shipping distributions (we go for "the versions of major distros still supported by the distro vendor", roughly), and we follow the usual/traditional C project/Linux distro approach of "build with the versions of libraries, dependencies and tools shipped by the build platform" generally. At the moment that means we need our docs to build with Sphinx versions ranging from 1.6 through to 3.2, and the concept of a "pinned version" just doesn't exist in this ecosystem. Being able to build with the distro version of Sphinx is made much more awkward if the documentation markup language is not a well specified and stable target for documentation authors to aim at.

Incidentally, the current documentation of the option:: directive in https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/restructuredtext/domains.html?highlight=option#directive-option says nothing about this requirement for -, --, / or +.

For the moment I've dealt with this by rewriting the fragment of documentation to avoid the option directive. I don't want to get into an argument if the Sphinx project doesn't feel that strong backward-compatibility guarantees are a project goal, so I thought I'd just write up my suggestions/hopes for sphinx-build more generally for you to consider (or reject!) and leave it at that:

* Where directives/markup have a required syntax for their arguments, it would be useful if the documentation clearly and precisely described the syntax. That allows documentation authors to know whether they're using something as intended.
* Where possible, the initial implementation should start with tightly parsing that syntax and diagnosing errors. It's much easier to loosen restrictions or use a previously forbidden syntax for a new purpose if older implementations just rejected it rather than if they accepted it and did something different because they didn't parse it very strictly.
* Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

and on a more general note I would appreciate it if the project considered the needs of external non-Python projects that have adopted Sphinx as a documentation system but which don't necessarily have the same control over tooling versions that Python-ecosystem projects might. (The Linux kernel is another good example here.)

> Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

Major versions are done via semver, where Sphinx 2 is a major breaking change over Sphinx 1, and Sphinx 3 breaks changes over Sphinx 2. What other things could be done? The concept of deprecation isn't as common in Python communities due to the popularity of fixed versions or locking to a major version. IE ``pip install sphinx==3`` which installs the latest major sphinx version of 3.
This change was added at https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/pull/7770. It is not an expected change. It means this is a mere bug.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc ignores explicit empty __all__ (documents all members)
problem_statement.Description: When a module defines __all__ = [] (an explicit empty export list), sphinx.ext.autodoc treats that as if no __all__ were defined and documents all members. The behavior appears with automodule using the :members: option.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Presence test conflates absent __all__ and explicit empty __all__ — file: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, class: ModuleDocumenter, method: get_object_members. The code calls inspect.getall(self.object) and stores the result in self.__all__ (ModuleDocumenter.import_object). inspect.getall(...) returns None when the module has no __all__ attribute and returns the sequence (which may be empty) when the module defines __all__. get_object_members currently tests presence using truthiness (if not self.__all__): this evaluates True both when self.__all__ is None (no __all__) and when self.__all__ == [] (explicit empty __all__). As a result, an explicit empty __all__ is treated like 'no __all__' and implicit member discovery is used, causing all members to be documented instead of none.

2) Same presence-test pattern used for ordering — file: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py, class: ModuleDocumenter, method: sort_members. The method checks 'if order == "bysource" and self.__all__:' (truthiness) to decide whether to apply __all__-based ordering; this likewise treats an explicit empty __all__ as absent. Both checks share the same incorrect assumption about how inspect.getall signals absence vs presence of __all__ (None vs a sequence).
StepsToReproduce:
Create index.rst containing:
.. automodule:: example
   :members:
Build the docs (e.g., sphinx-build . _build) and view the generated automodule output.
Observe that foo, bar, and baz are listed despite __all__ being an explicit empty list.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a module defines __all__ = [], autodoc should show no members for that module (an explicit empty __all__ means the module exports nothing).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: autodoc shows all module members (foo, bar, baz) as if the module had no __all__ defined.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc doesn't resolve struct.Struct type annotations (module prefix dropped)
problem_statement.Description: When a function or return annotation uses struct.Struct, Sphinx emits a cross-reference warning "class reference target not found: Struct" because the produced annotation text loses the module prefix and becomes "Struct" instead of "struct.Struct", so the Python domain cannot resolve it.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) sphinx.util.typing.stringify (sphinx/util/typing.py) and sphinx.util.typing.restify (same file) decide to omit the module prefix for a type solely based on the type object's __module__ attribute equaling 'builtins' (or '__builtin__'). Concretely, stringify contains the branch: elif (getattr(annotation, '__module__', None) == 'builtins' and hasattr(annotation, '__qualname__')): return annotation.__qualname__ (sphinx/util/typing.py). restify contains the branch: elif cls.__module__ in ('__builtin__', 'builtins'): return ':class:`%s`' % cls.__name__ (sphinx/util/typing.py). This encodes the assumption: "if __module__ indicates builtins, it is safe to omit the module prefix." 2) That assumption is fragile: it relies only on the __module__ string value, not on whether the object is actually defined in the builtins module object itself. If a type object has __module__ equal to 'builtins' (or '__builtin__') but is not actually present in builtins.__dict__, omitting the module produces an unqualified name (e.g., 'Struct') that the Python domain cannot resolve because the real qualified name is 'struct.Struct'. This exact fragile test also appears in sphinx.ext.inheritance_diagram (class_name) where module in ('__builtin__','builtins') is used to drop the module prefix (sphinx/ext/inheritance_diagram.py). 3) Because stringify/restify/class_name are the upstream producers of the textual form used to create pending_xref.reftarget, producing an unqualified text directly causes pending_xref to have an unresolvable reftarget (the downstream python domain parsing/resolution then fails and emits warnings such as "class reference target not found: Struct").
StepsToReproduce: Create a module helloworld.py containing:
import struct
import pathlib
def consume_struct(_: struct.Struct) -> None:
pass
def make_struct() -> struct.Struct:
mystruct = struct.Struct('HH')
return mystruct
def make_path() -> pathlib.Path:
return pathlib.Path()
Create a simple index.rst that automodules the above (.. automodule:: helloworld.helloworld).
Run Sphinx: python3 -m sphinx -b html docs/ doc-out -nvWT
Observe warning: "class reference target not found: Struct" and that struct.Struct links are not resolved.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Annotations that reference struct.Struct should be displayed and linked using the fully qualified name (i.e., 'struct.Struct') so that autodoc produces a resolvable cross-reference and the generated documentation contains a link or correct signature for struct.Struct.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Sphinx emits a cross-reference warning 'class reference target not found: Struct' because the produced annotation string lost the 'struct.' prefix (i.e., became 'Struct') and the Python domain cannot resolve that unqualified name.

hints_text:
Unfortunately, the `struct.Struct` class does not have the correct module-info. So it is difficult to support.
```
Python 3.8.2 (default, Mar  2 2020, 00:44:41)
[Clang 11.0.0 (clang-1100.0.33.17)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'builtins'
```

Note: In python3.9, it returns the correct module-info. But it answers the internal module name: `_struct`.
```
Python 3.9.1 (default, Dec 18 2020, 00:18:40)
[Clang 11.0.3 (clang-1103.0.32.59)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'_struct'
```

So it would better to use `autodoc_type_aliases` to correct it forcedly.

problem_statement.Title: napoleon_use_param ignored for "Other parameters" section
problem_statement.Description: The "Other parameters" section of NumPy-/Google-style docstrings is always formatted using the grouped/list style (a ':Other Parameters:' header with inline items) and does not honor the napoleon_use_param configuration option. This leads to inconsistent rendering between the Parameters section (which does respect napoleon_use_param) and the Other parameters section when napoleon_use_param=True.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py — GoogleDocstring._parse_other_parameters_section (lines 684-685): this method unconditionally calls _format_fields(_('Other Parameters'), self._consume_fields()). The code encodes the assumption that the "Other parameters" section must always be rendered with the grouped/list style and does not consult napoleon_use_param.

2) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py — GoogleDocstring._parse_parameters_section (lines 687-695): by contrast, the Parameters section checks self._config.napoleon_use_param and, when True, obtains fields via self._consume_fields(multiple=True) and passes them to self._format_docutils_params(fields). This is the behavior the Other parameters section does not follow.

3) Correct relationship (diagnostic statement preserving exact methods/values): when self._config.napoleon_use_param is True, parameter-like sections should obtain fields via self._consume_fields(multiple=True) (to allow comma-separated declarations like 'x, y: int') and pass those fields to self._format_docutils_params(fields) (which emits ':param <name>:' and ':type <name>: <type>' lines). When napoleon_use_param is False, these sections should produce grouped/list output by calling self._format_fields(<SectionLabel>, fields).

4) Why this produces the symptom: _format_docutils_params(...) produces docutils param/type directives (':param name:' and ':type name: <type>') while _format_fields(...) produces a header like ':Other Parameters:' and inline items (using ' * ' markers when multiple). Because _parse_other_parameters_section currently unconditionally uses _format_fields, napoleon_use_param has no effect on how Other parameters are rendered, resulting in the inconsistency described.
StepsToReproduce:
Create a NumPy-style docstring that contains both a Parameters section and an Other parameters section, for example:

Parameters
----------
x : int

Other parameters
----------------
y: float

Use sphinx.ext.napoleon.NumpyDocstring to parse/format that docstring and print the result, e.g.:

from sphinx.ext.napoleon import NumpyDocstring
print(str(NumpyDocstring("""
Parameters
----------
x : int

Other parameters
----------------
y: float
""")))

Observe that the Parameters section is formatted according to napoleon_use_param (':param x:' / ':type x: int' when True) while the Other parameters section is always rendered as a grouped field (':Other Parameters: **y** (*float*)').
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The "Other parameters" section should follow the same visible formatting rules as the "Parameters" section. Specifically, when napoleon_use_param is True, other parameters should be emitted as docutils ':param <name>:' and ':type <name>: <type>' entries (one per parameter). When napoleon_use_param is False, the section should be rendered in the grouped/list style (a header like 'Other Parameters' with inline items).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Regardless of the napoleon_use_param setting, the "Other parameters" section is rendered using the grouped/list style (e.g. ':Other Parameters: **y** (*float*)'), while the "Parameters" section respects napoleon_use_param (e.g. ':param x:' and ':type x: int' when napoleon_use_param is True).

problem_statement.Title: viewcode creates epub module pages even when viewcode_enable_epub=False
problem_statement.Description: When building epub (builders whose names start with 'epub'), the viewcode extension still generates module pages via the 'html-collect-pages' event even if the configuration option viewcode_enable_epub is False. The doctree_read handler already avoids inserting viewcode links for epub when viewcode_enable_epub is False, but collect_pages (registered on 'html-collect-pages') yields pages unconditionally, so module pages are created for epub output despite the config.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) collect_pages in sphinx/ext/viewcode.py is registered on the 'html-collect-pages' event (setup connects it) and yields module pages for any builder that emits that event; collect_pages does not check the current builder name nor env.config.viewcode_enable_epub. Location: sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::collect_pages (starting at line ~181). 2) StandaloneHTMLBuilder.gen_pages_from_extensions emits 'html-collect-pages' and calls handle_page for whatever pagelists the event handlers yield. Because EpubBuilder inherits StandaloneHTMLBuilder, it also emits that event and will therefore invoke collect_pages. Location: sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py::StandaloneHTMLBuilder::gen_pages_from_extensions (lines ~642–646). 3) doctree_read already guards against EPUB output by returning early when app.builder.name.startswith('epub') and not env.config.viewcode_enable_epub; collect_pages lacks the same guard, causing an inconsistency between link insertion and page generation. Location: sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::doctree_read (lines ~57–60) vs sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::collect_pages (no comparable guard). 4) Relevant hardcoded constants and their meanings (preserved as-is): OUTPUT_DIRNAME = '_modules' (folder for module pages; sphinx/ext/viewcode.py line 32), StandaloneHTMLBuilder.out_suffix = '.html' (default HTML suffix; sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py), and EpubBuilder sets out_suffix = '.xhtml' in its init (sphinx/builders/_epub_base.py lines ~145–148). These constants are correct — the defect is the missing runtime check in collect_pages. 5) Correct relationship that must hold (diagnostic statement): collect_pages must respect the same rule used by doctree_read — i.e., when app.builder.name.startswith('epub') and env.config.viewcode_enable_epub is False, collect_pages must not yield module pages (it should return without generating pages).
StepsToReproduce: 1) Ensure conf.py has 'sphinx.ext.viewcode' in extensions and viewcode_enable_epub = False (default).
2) Run a build that includes an epub builder, e.g. 'make epub' or the Makefile target 'make html epub' (so an epub builder run occurs).

3) Inspect the epub output directory (e.g. _build/epub) or the generated epub manifest: viewcode module pages appear under _modules/ (or listed in content.opf) even though viewcode_enable_epub is False.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When viewcode_enable_epub is False, an epub build should not produce viewcode module pages; the epub output (files and manifest) should not contain viewcode-generated module pages or references.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Even with viewcode_enable_epub=False, module pages are produced for epub builds (collect_pages yields pages under _modules/ with builder-specific suffixes, e.g. .xhtml) because collect_pages runs for any builder that emits 'html-collect-pages' and does not check the viewcode_enable_epub configuration.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc: inherited annotation-only attribute in superclass treated as undocumented
problem_statement.Description: When documenting a subclass with ".. autoclass:: <SubClass>" using :members: and :inherited-members:, an attribute defined only by an annotation (PEP 526) in a superclass (and annotated with an inline #: comment) is omitted from the generated docs unless :undoc-members: is also given. The missing attribute is treated as "undocumented" even though the source-level inline comment exists.
problem_statement.RootCause:
Multiple member-discovery code paths create member placeholders and then refuse to merge analyzer-provided attribute documentation or annotation-only metadata into an already-present entry. Concretely:

1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py::get_class_members — merging gated by membership: the function creates members from several discovery paths (dir(), __slots__, enum, etc.). Later it adds annotation-only entries and analyzer.attr_docs entries only when the name is not already in members (the code uses checks of the form "if name and name not in members" and "if ns == qualname and name not in members"). Example lines: the annotation-only pass and analyzer pass use "if name and name not in members:" and "if ns == qualname and name not in members:" (importer.py). Because of this gating, if an earlier discovery path created a members[name] placeholder without docstring and/or without .class_ owner metadata, the analyzer-provided docstring and/or the annotation-only ownership are never attached to that existing ObjectMember.

2) Same root pattern elsewhere in importer helper functions — the identical "only add when missing" pattern appears in other importer functions that gather members:
   - sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py::get_object_members uses analyzer.find_attr_docs() but only creates Attribute entries when the name is not already present ("if namespace == ns and name not in members:").
   - sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py::get_module_members (deprecated helper) likewise adds annotation-only names only when missing ("if name not in members:").
   These locations share the same failure-to-merge behavior: analyzer attribute docs or annotation-only metadata are ignored when a prior discovery created a placeholder without metadata.

3) Consumer expectations in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::Documenter.filter_members — reliance on ObjectMember metadata: the filtering logic uses ObjectMember.docstring (it overrides getdoc(member) with obj.docstring when present) to compute has_doc, and uses ObjectMember.class_ in the inherited-member checks. Example lines: the code does "if isinstance(obj, ObjectMember) and obj.docstring: doc = obj.docstring" and the inherited-member helper checks "elif isinstance(obj, ObjectMember) and obj.class_ is cls:". Because get_class_members (or other importer helpers) may leave an existing ObjectMember without docstring and/or without class_, filter_members will treat the member as undocumented (has_doc == False) and/or be unable to determine its declaring class, causing inherited annotation-only attributes with inline #: comments to be omitted unless :undoc-members: is supplied.

4) Data model / sentinel interaction — INSTANCEATTR: the code represents annotation-only attributes with the INSTANCEATTR sentinel. Because analyzer.attr_docs and annotation-only detection are only used to create new members when the name is missing, the INSTANCEATTR sentinel (or a placeholder) attached earlier to a member may lack the analyzer docstring and class_ owner expected by filter_members. The correct invariant expected by consumers is: when analyzer.attr_docs contains a doc for (qualname, name), the canonical members entry used by Documenter.filter_members must have its .docstring set to that value and its .class_ set to the defining class (qualname), regardless of whether a members entry was previously created. Existing real runtime objects should not be discarded; instead, metadata (docstring and class_) must be merged into the member entry so that downstream filtering and display logic can treat the attribute as documented and owned by the defining class.
StepsToReproduce: Create a file example.py with:
class Foo:
"""docstring"""
attr1: int  #: docstring
class Bar(Foo):
"""docstring"""
attr2: str  #: docstring
Create index.rst with:
.. autoclass:: example.Bar
:members:
:inherited-members:
Build the docs (sphinx-build) and observe that Bar.attr1 is not shown. It appears only if :undoc-members: is added.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When documenting Bar with :members: and :inherited-members:, the inherited annotation-only attribute Foo.attr1 (with its inline #: comment) should be listed under Bar and its inline docstring should be displayed — no :undoc-members: option should be required.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Foo.attr1 (annotation-only attribute defined in the superclass) is omitted from Bar's documented members and treated as undocumented; it only appears if the :undoc-members: option is added.
